title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardians of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream / Te Mahi Kaitiaki mō Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga (guardianship) through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will connect with Ngāti Kahungunu traditions of environmental stewardship while addressing modern pollution challenges affecting our local waterway.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret traditional Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about water guardianship to identify key cultural values
Create persuasive texts (letters, speeches, or presentations) advocating for the protection of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Collaborate effectively in groups to research, discuss, and propose solutions to local environmental issues
Use appropriate language features and structures to communicate ideas clearly to different audiences (whānau, community, local council)

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Stream: Connecting to Place
activities.description: Students listen to local kaumātua share pūrākau about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. They then create visual mind maps linking the stories to values of kaitiakitanga, using both English and Te Reo Māori terms.

activities.title: Voice of the Water: Persuasive Writing Workshop
activities.description: Using water quality data from recent regional council reports on our stream, students craft persuasive letters to local decision-makers. They learn rhetorical strategies and incorporate cultural references to strengthen their arguments for protection measures.

activities.title: Community Voices: Interviewing Kaitiaki
activities.description: In small groups, students develop interview questions and conduct recorded interviews (in person or via video) with local environmental kaitiaki from Ngāti Kahungunu environmental trusts. They practice active listening and note-taking skills.

activities.title: Solutions Symposium: Presenting Our Mahi
activities.description: Groups create multimedia presentations proposing specific actions to protect Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, incorporating their research, interview findings, and cultural understanding. They present to an audience including whānau and community representatives.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and representatives from Te Taiwhenua o Heretaunga environmental team
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' reports specific to the Mangakōtukutuku catchment
Digital recording equipment for interviews and presentation creation
'He Pūrākau o Te Awa' - local story collections from the Hastings District Library Māori heritage section

tasks: Written persuasive letter to local council incorporating cultural and environmental evidence
Recorded group presentation proposing specific kaitiakitanga actions for the stream
Reflective journal documenting learning journey and personal connection to place

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies; Ideas; Language features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and strategies; Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Health and Physical Education: Community resources; Interpersonal skills
